coredata.dc:title: Histamine-responsive dye-incorporated carbon dots for visual monitoring of food spoilage
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Yujuan
Huang, Pengcheng
Wu, Fang-Ying
coredata.dc:description: With food safety problems abounding, it is essential to explore some efficient and effective methods for monitoring food quality. To this end, we have developed a histamine-responsive carbon dots (CDs)-based sensing system for visual monitoring of food spoilage. In this system, the aldehyde group on the surface of CDs serves as the recognition sites to combine with the amino group on histamine to form a Schiff base that effectively enhances the fluorescence. On the other hand, 1-aminoanthraquinone (ANQ) was introduced as a reference signal to endow the fluorescent probe with self-calibrating capacity. As the histamine concentration increases, linear ratiometric response was obtained accompanied with a distinguishable fluorescence color change from red to blue. Besides, test strips-based smartphone visual assays were successfully used to evaluate and monitor the histamine levels in fish samples. Our proposed sensing strategy has been demonstrated to be efficient and robust along with great potential for real-time visual monitoring of food spoilage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Histamine detection
Test strips
Color analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the CDs and CDs@ANQ
Visual quantification of histamine based on CDs@ANQ loaded test strips
Detection of histamine in actual samples
Characterization of the CDs and CDs@ANQ
Visual detection and analysis of histamine in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of CDs. (b) Size distribution histograms of CDs. (c) XRD patterns of CDs. (d) FT-IR spectra of OPA and CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and OPA. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at various excitation wavelengths (300–400 nm).

Characterization of CDs@ANQ: (a) Fluorescence spectra, and (b) UV–vis absorption spectra. Inset of (a) shows the corresponding images of the CDs (A), ANQ (B), and CDs@ANQ (C) under a 365 nm UV lamp. (c) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@ANQ with various volume ratios of CDs to ANQ.

Fluorescence spectra of (a) CDs@ANQ and (d) free CDs after the addition of different amounts of histamine (0–20 µM). The relationship between the fluorescence intensity against histamine concentration: (b) CDs@ANQ and (e) free CDs. The insets show fluorescent fluorescence photographs of the corresponding sensing system. CIE chromaticity diagrams of (c) CDs@ANQ and (f) free CDs with different amounts of histamine.
(a) Chemical structures of BAs used in this study. (b) Effect of various BAs on the fluorescence characteristics of CDs@ANQ.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra after the addition of histamine. (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs, histamine, and CDs + histamine.

(a) Fluorescence intensity change of CDs solutions at different pH values. (b) Fluorescence responses of CDs toward common interferences (from a to z: blank, histamine, K+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, HS-, S2-, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, NO2 -, NO3 -, CO3 2-, Ser, Cys, Asp, Met, Arg, His, Try, Thr, Glu, Tyr, GSH, BSA. Concentrations: histamine and histidine, 5 μM; others, 20 μM except BSA (2 μg/mL)).

(a) Fluorescence color change of test strips under a 365 nm UV lamp with increasing histamine levels and RGB analysis of the fluorescence image via a Color Analyzer APP in the smartphone. (b) The correlation between B/R and histamine concentrations corresponding to (a). (c) Fluorescence images of test strips impregnated by histamine extracted from grass carp under different storage conditions.
Illustration of histamine detection based on CDs@ANQ sensor and the CDs@ANQ-loaded test strips for visual monitoring of food spoilage.

head.ce:title: Histamine-responsive dye-incorporated carbon dots for visual monitoring of food spoilage

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With food safety problems abounding, it is essential to explore some efficient and effective methods for monitoring food quality. To this end, we have developed a histamine-responsive carbon dots (CDs)-based sensing system for visual monitoring of food spoilage. In this system, the aldehyde group on the surface of CDs serves as the recognition sites to combine with the amino group on histamine to form a Schiff base that effectively enhances the fluorescence. On the other hand, 1-aminoanthraquinone (ANQ) was introduced as a reference signal to endow the fluorescent probe with self-calibrating capacity. As the histamine concentration increases, linear ratiometric response was obtained accompanied with a distinguishable fluorescence color change from red to blue. Besides, test strips-based smartphone visual assays were successfully used to evaluate and monitor the histamine levels in fish samples. Our proposed sensing strategy has been demonstrated to be efficient and robust along with great potential for real-time visual monitoring of food spoilage.

ce:section.ce:para: Food and food safety are related to public health. Particularly, biogenic amines (BAs) are mainly generated through food aging and the action of microorganisms during food storage [1–4]. BAs widely exist in many foods and are considered important indicators for monitoring food quality [5,6]. Histamine has been extensively studied as one of the important BAs. The proper dose of histamine in the human body is conducive to many physiological processes, but excessive intake can pose toxicological effects like headaches, vomiting, and diarrhea [7–9]. Besides, the histamine level in protein-rich foods such as aquatic products and meat products is closely related to their quality and is expected to be an important indicator for evaluating the freshness of such foods [10,11]. Accordingly, given food safety and human health, exploring reliable and efficient strategies for the detection of BAs especially histamine is urgent and indispensable.

At present, multiple analytical methods including chromatography, electrochemistry, colorimetry, and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay have been developed to detect histamine [11–13]. However, large consumption of reagents, sophisticated and time-consuming operations, and expensive instrumentations are some of the troubles [3,12,14]. In contrast, fluorescent sensors are uniquely attractive due to high sensitivity, easy operation, and low cost [15]. Recently, different types of fluorescent sensors for the detection of BAs including histamine have been reported, including small organic molecules [13,16–20] and nanomaterials [9,15,21,22]. However, most of them suffer from some drawbacks. For example, some molecular fluorescent probes required tedious synthesis processes, and false-positive signals could be produced since single fluorescence change would be easily impacted by environmental factors [13]. Moreover, among these probes, fluorescent responses were mainly realized based on weak basicity of BAs. It may be interfered by complex biological matrices especially when BAs are at low concentrations in the initial stage of food spoilage, leading to poor selectivity and sensitivity. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a histamine-responsive ratiometric fluorescent probe featuring simple synthesis, good specificity, high sensitivity, and ease of visualization.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are known as quasi-spherical carbon nanomaterials with a size less than 10 nm. CDs have been significantly developed due to their superior advantages involving plentiful precursors, facile preparation, splendid biocompatibility, and fluorescence tunability [23–25]. These traits bestow CDs with broader application prospects in many fields such as sensing, cell imaging, catalysis, and optoelectronic devices [26–28]. Excitingly, diverse precursors endow the surface of CDs with rich functional groups, making them tend to functionalize and possess special molecular recognition capabilities that are extremely attractive in sensing studies. Wei et al. reported a CDs-based sensor that enabled a rapid, reversible, dynamic response to hypochlorite and ascorbic acid [29]. Bhattacharya et al. developed a fast, sensitive nitric oxide (NO) sensor based on CDs and achieved real-time NO detection in cells [30].

In this work, we have explored a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for histamine by integrating CDs and an inert red-emitting fluorescent dye (1-aminoanthraquinone, ANQ) (CDs@ANQ). Blue fluorescent CDs are prepared from o-phthalaldehyde by the solvothermal method. The formation of Schiff bases from aldehyde groups on the surface of CDs and amino groups on histamine can significantly enhance the fluorescence of CDs. Therefore, the marriage of CDs and the fluorescent dye constitutes a color-resolvable and highly selective recognition system. Based on the dual-emission response mechanism, the CDs@ANQ turn-on ratiometric fluorescence sensor we constructed exhibits superior sensitivity (LOD = 0.036 µM) and is highly specific for detecting histamine. Furthermore, a portable sensing platform is developed by loading CDs@ANQ onto the test strips and by integrating with a smartphone. The established sensing platform is successfully exploited for estimating histamine levels in foods and visual monitoring of food spoilage ( Scheme 1).

o-phthalaldehyde (OPA), 1-aminoanthraquinone (ANQ), histamine, spermine, spermidine, tyramine, tryptamine, cadaverine, serine (Ser), cysteine (Cys), aspartic acid (Asp), methionine (Met), arginine (Arg), histidine (His), tryptophan (Try), threonine (Thr), glutamate (Glu), tyrosine (Tyr), glutathione (GSH), and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Aladdin Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). KCl, CaCl2, CuCl2, MgCl2, ZnCl2, NaHS, Na2S, Na2SO3, Na2SO4, NaNO2, NaNO3, Na2CO3, and p-Nitroaniline were obtained Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 instrument. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement was obtained on a Nano ZS90 zetasizer (Malvern, UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectra were recorded via X-ray diffractometer (Panalytical) utilizing Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 30 mA, λ = 0.154 nm) and LabRam HR spectrometer (Horiba-Jobin). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded by ESCALAB250Xi photoelectron spectrometer. Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a BRUKER Tensor II spectrometer. Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer and Shimadzu-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer, respectively. The absolute quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime were determined by the FLS1000 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the CDs and CDs@ANQ
ce:section.ce:para: 1.5 mmol of OPA was mixed evenly in 30 mL of ethanol, transferred to Teflon autoclave, and heated to 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling naturally, the crude products were processed by centrifugation (10, 000 rpm, 15 min) and filtration (0.22 µm filter membrane). And then the solution was further purified through silica column chromatography. The purified CDs were collected for further experiments by removing solvents and later drying under a vacuum. Briefly, CDs@ANQ was synthesized by mixing 0.05 mg·mL−1 CDs ethanol solution with 0.223 mg mL−1 ANQ ethanol solution (the volume ratio is 3:1) and sonicated at room temperature for 30 min

For histamine detection, various concentrations of histamine aqueous solution were introduced into 1 mL of CDs@ANQ ethanol solution, and the final total volume was maintained at 2 mL. After homogeneous mixing and complete reaction for 100 min, the corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded with 360 nm excitation wavelength. The same experimental procedures were performed in other BAs (spermine, spermidine, tyramine, tryptamine, cadaverine).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual quantification of histamine based on CDs@ANQ loaded test strips
ce:section.ce:para: The common filter paper was cut into small circular pieces by a 16 mm perforator, immersed in CDs@ANQ solution, and removed to dry naturally to obtain the test strips. 30 µL histamine solutions with different concentrations were added dropwise to the test strips. The fluorescent color of the test strips was then recorded by a smartphone under a UV lamp with an incidence of 45°. The smartphone is located 20 cm directly above the test strips and the UV lamp is about 25 cm away from the test strips. The “Color Analyzer” app is used to analyze the color change of the test strips and extract the corresponding RGB (red, green, blue) values. Through data processing, the standard curve of B/R ratio and histamine content can be obtained for visual quantitative analysis of histamine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of histamine in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Fish samples (grass carp, crucian) were bought from a local supermarket in Nanchang, China. The sample processing method is as follows: 10 g of homogenate sample and 20 mL of ultrapure water were added to mix well for 5 min. After being treated by centrifugation (10, 000 rpm, 10 min) and filtration, the supernatant was stored at 4 °C for the following detection [14,31].

In the spike-in experiments, a given concentration of histamine solution was added to the above sample. For the solution-based ratiometric fluorescence detection, we needed to dilute the corresponding sample solution to ensure that the histamine concentration is in the range of 0.2–20 µM, while for test strips-based smartphone measurement, the sample solution was added dropwise directly to detect the histamine content. The accuracy of the experimental results was further verified by using the UV–vis spectrophotometric method (Chinese Standard GB 5009.208–2016). Briefly, the histamine standard solution or sample solution was quantified after a chromogenic reaction with an azo reagent (the mixed solution of p-nitroaniline and sodium nitrite).

To further evaluate the feasibility of our proposed method in monitoring sample spoilage, the fish samples were stored at three different temperatures (−20 °C, 4 °C, and 25 °C) for 3 days. After the pretreatment as above, the sample solution was diluted 100-fold for the solution-based fluorescence analysis. The undiluted sample solution was dropped directly onto the test strips for the smartphone-based measurement. The histamine content was also further verified by the UV–vis spectrophotometric method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CDs and CDs@ANQ
ce:section.ce:para: We optimized reaction conditions to obtain CDs with superior fluorescence properties. As depicted in Fig. S1, the optimal conditions for the synthesis of CDs are 1.5 mmol OPA, heating at 180 °C for 12 h, respectively.

The particle size and morphology of CDs are characterized through TEM. The CDs present a uniformly dispersed spherical state with an average particle size of 2.09 ± 0.02 nm ( Fig. 1). From the HRTEM image, it is clear that the CDs have lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.30 nm, which is assigned to the (111) plane of the graphitic carbon [32]. The size of the CDs obtained from DLS measurement is in general agreement with the TEM result (Fig. S2a). The XRD pattern showing a broad diffraction peak at around 24° further confirms the crystallinity of the CDs (Fig. 1c). The Raman spectra of the CDs demonstrate a D band at 1352 cm−1 and a G band at 1595 cm−1, which are associated with sp3-hybridized carbon atoms of disordered graphite and the sp2-bonded carbon atoms, respectively (Fig. S2b) [33].

The elemental composition and structure of CDs are explored by FT-IR and XPS. The comparison of the FT-IR spectra of OPA and CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1d. OPA displays characteristic absorption peaks at 2853 cm−1 and 2756 cm−1 (i.e., the C-H bending vibration on the aldehyde group) and 1700 cm−1 (i.e., the CO stretching vibration on the aldehyde group). Compared with OPA, the apparent enhancement of the =C-H stretching vibration at 3050 cm−1 corresponding to the aromatic ring from OPA to CDs might be ascribed to the larger conjugated degree of the synthesized CDs [34]. CDs exhibit distinct absorption peaks located at 2750 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1 are coincident with the C-H and CO stretching vibrations of the aldehyde group. As shown in Fig. S3a, the XPS full spectrum reveals that CDs are composed of carbon and oxygen elements. The C 1 s spectrum is deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.7 eV (CC/C-C), 286.6 eV (C-O), and 288 eV (CO) [35] with a relative proportion of 87.11%, 7.09%, and 5.80%, respectively (Fig. S3b). The high-resolution spectrum of O 1s can be fitted into three peaks at 531 eV (quinone CO), 531.6 eV (carbonyl CO), and 532.5 eV (C-O), respectively [36] (Fig. S3c). The proportions of three different oxygen moieties are 0.59%, 65.17%, and 34.24%, respectively, implying the majority of oxygen was bonded to carbon in the form of CO. These analysis results indicate that the surface of the CDs may be covered with oxygen-containing functional groups such as aldehyde groups.

The optical properties of the CDs have also been explored. As illustrated in Fig. 2a, the precursor OPA exhibits two absorption peaks at 250 nm and 300 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of the CC bond and the n-π* transition of the CO bond. The CDs also have similar absorption peaks compared to OPA. the absorption peak located at 230 and 255 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition of CC bands. The broad absorption band around 303 nm can be ascribed to the n-π* transition of CO bands of the aldehyde group on the surface of CDs [37,38]. Fig. 2b reveals that CDs possess excitation-dependent characteristics under various excitation wavelengths, which may be attributed to the multiple emitting centers and different energy level distribution [39]. The absolute fluorescence QY and an average lifetime of CDs are calculated to be 8.3% and 4.76 ns, respectively. Additionally, the CDs manifested good photostability under long-term storage at 4 °C (15 days) (Fig. S4).

To achieve the visual analysis and overcome the drawbacks of single-wavelength fluorescence, we constructed a ratiometric fluorescent sensor CDs@ANQ by introducing ANQ as an internal reference. As presented in Fig. 3a, the CDs@ANQ displays two distinguishable emission peaks at 430 nm and 610 nm, corresponding to CDs and ANQ, respectively. CDs@ANQ exhibits orange-red fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, which differs from the blue fluorescence of CDs and the red fluorescence of ANQ. The strong absorption peaks of CDs@ANQ are consistent with the absorption bands of CDs (303 nm) and ANQ (480 nm) (Fig. 3b). Notably, no new absorption peaks emerge, indicating that CDs did not react with ANQ, which is probably because of large steric hindrance of ANQ. In addition, the absorption band of ANQ has overlapped well with the emission peak of CDs. With the increase of the fraction of ANQ in CDs@ANQ, the fluorescence at 430 nm has progressively weakened, while the fluorescence at 610 nm gradually increased along with a slight blue shift of the emission peak (Fig. 3c). This change may be ascribed to the energy transfer between CDs and ANQ [40,41]. We further investigated the variation in fluorescence lifetime. CDs present a reduced lifetime after the addition of ANQ, while the lifetime of ANQ showed a slight change (Table S1). The reduced fluorescence lifetime manifests a dynamic quenching process between the CDs and ANQ. It is reported that the energy transfer generally a dynamic quenching mechanism with a changing fluorescence lifetime [42]. Therefore, the quenched fluorescence of CDs by ANQ mainly results from the energy transfer process.

To obtain the best sensing performance, we investigated the effect of some sensing parameters. We first explored the effect of CDs with different concentrations. As shown in Fig. S5a, 0.05 mg/mL has been selected as the best concentration. Subsequently, the volume ratio of CDs to ANQ is optimized as 3:1 (Fig. S5b). The fluorescence intensity ratio (I430/I610) of CDs@ANQ reaches equilibrium within 100 min in the presence of histamine (Fig. S5c).

Under the optimal reaction conditions, we further evaluated the sensing performance of CDs@ANQ toward histamine. The changes in fluorescence spectra of CDs@ANQ with the addition of different histamine concentrations are displayed in Fig. 4a. Upon increasing histamine, the fluorescence of CDs at 430 nm gradually increases whereas that of ANQ shows a relatively stable emission at 610 nm. The fluorescence intensity ratio I430/I610 is linearly correlated with the histamine concentration (0.2–20 µM) (Fig. 4b). The limit of detection (LOD) for histamine is as low as 0.036 µM based on the formula LOD = 3S0/k (S0 is the standard deviation of the blank experiment and k is the slope of the linear correlation curve). Meanwhile, an obvious transition in the fluorescent color from orange-red to violet can be readily identified by the naked eyes. The CIE chromaticity has also validated the reliability of color variations in the sensing system (Fig. 4c).

We further applied free CDs as the control to perform single-signal fluorescent detection of histamine (Fig. 4d–f). The fluorescence of CDs at 430 nm gradually enhances with increasing histamine concentration. It has revealed a favorable linear correlation to the histamine over a range of 0.5–20 µM, and the LOD is calculated to be 0.319 µM. However, in this case, single fluorescence intensity changes and unchangeable chromaticity coordinates are quite difficult to visually distinguish from histamine at different concentrations. The detection accuracy is also susceptible to environmental conditions and influences of instruments. Thereby, the turn-on ratiometric fluorescence sensor we constructed possesses better visualization capability and sensitivity.

We also examined the fluorescence responses of CDs@ANQ to other common BAs (tyramine, tryptamine, cadaverine, spermine, and spermidine). As shown in Fig. 5, the CDs@ANQ have different sensitivities to different BAs, and the response to histamine is the most dramatic. These results illustrate that this method has more satisfactory sensitivity toward histamine than other BAs. Compared with previously reported methods for histamine detection, our proposed method has many advantages such as ease of preparation, better visualization capability, and excellent sensitivity (Table S2).

We conducted a series of studies to gain insight into the potential mechanism of the presented sensor for histamine. We first ruled out the possibility of the interaction between ANQ and histamine. As shown in Fig. S6, ANQ is virtually insensitive to histamine. So, we paid attention to the combination between CDs and histamine. As depicted in Fig. 6a, upon adding histamine to the CDs solution, the π-π* transition peak is broadened and a new absorption peak appears at 350 nm. This may be attributed to the formation of imines (CN) between the aldehyde groups on CDs and the amino groups on histamine [36]. In the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 6b), the characteristic peaks at 2750 cm−1and 1700 cm−1 indicate that there are residual aldehyde groups on the surface of CDs. Following the response of CDs toward histamine, the aldehyde signal disappears and the characteristic peak of imine at 1650 cm−1 is generated [37,43]. The characteristic peak at 1094 cm−1 corresponding to C-N of histamine is also significantly weakened. These results demonstrate that the -CHO groups on the surface of CDs form a Schiff base with the -NH2 group on histamine. Moreover, the changes in the XPS spectra after CDs bound to histamine also confirmed the speculation. Both the content (from 25.3% to 15.4%, at%) and the intensity of O 1s decrease after the introduction of histamine into CDs, which proved the consumption of aldehyde groups (Fig. S7a and c) [44,45]. The high-resolution N 1s spectra reveal a characteristic peak at 397.8 eV assigned to the CN group (Fig. S7b). The XPS results fall in line with the speculation that the imine structure is formed upon the reaction of CDs and histamine.

We next explored the mechanism of fluorescence turn-on for histamine detection. It is found that the fluorescence lifetime of CDs at 430 nm increased from 4.07 ns to 6.4 ns while that of ANQ at 610 nm exhibited a slight reduction upon addition of histamine (Table S3). This may be due to the reaction of CDs with histamine to form Schiff base that attenuates the energy transfer process between CDs and ANQ. Meanwhile, the fluorescence turn-on response of CDs to histamine is associated with the formation of imine bonds. The extended conjugation system formed by the Schiff base, as well as the π-π interaction between the benzene ring on CDs and the imidazole on histamine both facilitates the fluorescence enhancement [15,46,47].

As mentioned above, many previous reports have been developed based on weak basicity of BAs, in which false-positive results could be obtained due to slight changes of biological microenvironment. However, in our work, the Schiff base formation between CDs and histamine well circumvents this issue. As shown in Fig. 7a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs has no obvious change in a broad pH range from 3 to 10. This demonstrates that CDs are insensitive to basic environments, thus providing the application potential in complex biological samples. As exhibited in Fig. 7b, common biological species including ions, amino acids, and proteins have negligible effects on the fluorescence of CDs. It should be noted that histidine shows a similar effect to histamine, which is mainly owing to their similar structures [47]. This further suggests that specific reaction between CDs and histamine makes our method applicable in complicated biosystems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection and analysis of histamine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the outstanding sensing properties of CDs@ANQ, we further explored its potential as a portable test strips-based sensor. As exhibited in Fig. 8a, the noticeable color transition from red to blue can be observed upon increasing the histamine concentration. The color signal is converted to the B/R ratio value via a Color Analyzer application, and a good linear relationship (R=0.987) between the B/R value and the histamine concentrations is acquired (Fig. 8b). Comparatively, the CDs@ANQ-loaded test strips themselves did not show a clear fluorescence color change under the same condition (Fig. S8). Therefore, by combining CDs@ANQ-loaded test strips with a smartphone, we have achieved convenient and rapid visual quantification of histamine.

To verify the practical application capability of the sensor, fish samples spiked with certain concentrations of histamine were analyzed. The results are listed in Table S4, and the spiked recoveries and RSD were 86.8–108% and 1.91–8.62% for the ratiometric fluorescence sensing. Correspondingly, the fluorescent color of the test strips has changed dramatically from purple to dark blue (Fig. S9). The analytical results are in general agreement with the UV–vis spectrophotometric method (Chinese Standard GB 5009.208–2016), indicating that our proposed strategy is accurate and feasible for sensing application in complex actual samples.

Fish samples can produce and accumulate histamine during processing and storage, so histamine is regarded as one of the important indicators for evaluating food quality. We finally monitored the dynamic changes of histamine levels in fish samples in real-time at different temperatures (−20 °C, 4 °C, and 25 °C). The histamine content of the sample varied over time at different temperatures. The histamine in the sample was extracted and the content changes were monitored by the solution-based and test strips-based fluorescence detection. As seen in Fig. 8c and Fig. S10, the histamine concentration significantly increased at 4 °C and 25 °C, and the fluorescence color of the test strips changed from purplish-red to purple (4 °C) and dark-blue (25 °C), indicating that histamine was released rapidly at higher temperatures. But for samples stored at low temperature (−20 °C), the histamine content changed slowly and can maintain freshness for a long time. The fluorescence color of the test strips changed to purple after three days of storage. The above two analytical methods were in good agreement with the UV–vis spectrophotometric results. The results demonstrate that our developed sensing platform has great potential for application in the field of intuitive identification of food spoilage.

In summary, blue fluorescent CDs were synthesized by the solvothermal method in this work. The specific interaction between the aldehyde group on CDs and the amine group on the histamine enables highly sensitive detection of histamine. Unlike conventional pH-responsive sensors for BAs, we have developed an activatable nanoprobe to achieve excellent selectivity and sensitivity toward histamine. The red-emitting fluorescent dye ANQ is integrated with CDs to construct a turn-on ratiometric sensing system with highly distinguishable fluorescent colors. The low-cost and portable test strips combined with a smartphone have also been successfully applied to the quantitative analysis and monitoring of dynamic changes of histamine levels in real samples, which would provide an efficient strategy for visual monitoring and assessment of food quality.
Yujuan Qin: Data curation, Software, Writing – original draft. Pengcheng Huang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Fang-Ying Wu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There is no conflict of interest for this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21864018, and 22164013), and Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (No. 20212BAB203033).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131911.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amine-responsive cellulose-based ratiometric fluorescent materials for real-time and visual detection of shrimp and crab freshness

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Jia, W. Tian, H. Bai, J. Zhang, S. Wang, J. Zhang, Amine-responsive cellulose-based ratiometric fluorescent materials for real-time and visual detection of shrimp and crab freshness, Nat. Commun. 10 (2019) 795. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467–019-08675–3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of biogenic amines in several foods with different sample treatments: An overview

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Vasconcelos, J.M.M.M. de Almeida, A. Matias, C. Saraiva, P.A.S. Jorge, L.C.C. Coelho, Detection of biogenic amines in several foods with different sample treatments: An overview, Trends Food Sci. Tech. 113 (2021) 86–96. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tifs.2021.04.043.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Designing an intelligent nanofiber ratiometric fluorescent sensor sensitive to biogenic amines for detecting the freshness of shrimp and pork

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Quan, H. He, H. Zhou, Y. Liang, L. Wang, S. Tian, H. Zhu, S. Wang, Designing an intelligent nanofiber ratiometric fluorescent sensor sensitive to biogenic amines for detecting the freshness of shrimp and pork, Sens. Actuators, B 333 (2021) 129535. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129535.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Intelligent food packaging: a review of smart sensing technologies for monitoring food quality

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Yousefi, H.-M. Su, S.M. Imani, K. Alkhaldi, C.D.M. Filipe, T.F. Didar, Intelligent food packaging: A review of smart sensing technologies for monitoring food quality, ACS Sens. 4 (2019) 808–821. https://doi.org/10.1021/acssensors.9b00440.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A hybrid upconversion nanoprobe for ratiometric detection of aliphatic biogenic amines in aqueous medium

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Jaiswal, S. Kundu, S. Bandyopadhyay, A. Patra, A hybrid upconversion nanoprobe for ratiometric detection of aliphatic biogenic amines in aqueous medium, Nanoscale Adv. 3 (2021) 3232–3239. https://doi.org/10.1039/D0NA00995D.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Couple of histamine blue fluorescence chemosensor and surface charge selector of FC-modified silica nanoporous for highly specific histamine detection via FRET-process

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Pengcheng Huang is currently an associate professor of College of Chemistry, Nanchang University, China. He received his B.Sc. and Ph.D. degree from Northwest University (2009) and Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences (2014), respectively. His current research focuses on nanomaterials-based fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for biomolecules.

Fang-Ying Wu is currently a professor of College of Chemistry, Nanchang University, China. She received her B.Sc. and M.Sc. degrees from Jiujiang Normal College (1987) and Nanchang University (1996), respectively. She obtained her Ph.D. in Xiamen University in 2003 and pursued her postdoctoral studies at Department of Chemistry, Seoul National University (2005–2006). Her work mainly focuses on design, synthesis, and related applications of novel organic dyes- and nanomaterials-based fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for metal ions, anions, and biomolecules.
